content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Prettiest movies?

**Score:** 39
**Body:** Buying a new TV soon, and I'd love to test it with a movie, as I've been saving for the TV for a while and feel like I should treat myself a little. So please give the prettiest, most colorful movie you can think of. Something that will really shine on a high-res TV.

  
Since "pretty" is sort of generic, I'll add that I love queerness as a theme, am super down to watch animation, enjoy fantasy worlds and all the like.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Crouching Tiger Hidden Dragon! Stunning visuals
  - Hero.
    - House of Flying Daggers
2. The Fall (2006)
3. I always recommend “The Fountain” (2006) for testing out a new tv. So beautiful, and fits your criteria of fantasy

But since it doesn’t have a 4k release yet (this year is the 20th anniversary so 🤞):

Three Thousand Years of Longing (2022)

Or

Frankenstein (2025)
4. Blade Runner 2049 has beautiful lighting and even won an Oscar for it
5. The Lord of the Rings trilogy really are some of the most beautiful movies you can watch.
6. Speed racer 

Because It's been on my mind and I want to watch it again: The Green Knight
7. Call Me by Your Name

Portrait of a Lady on Fire

Moonlight

Queer

The Darjeeling Limited
8. The Red Shoes hasn't been suggested yet, so I'll add that. And maybe Fantasia, if you're into classical music.

## 2. Looking for more dog movies 🐕

**Score:** 28
**Body:** Hi, I love dogs and dog movies (not the animated ones). I have watched the following so far:

1. The Call of the Wild
2. Togo
3. Benji
4. Hachi: A dog’s Tale
5. Marley & Me
6. A Dog’s Purpose

Please recommend others. Not necessarily English, can be foreign movie too, but definitely not animated.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Homeward Bound
2. Turner and Hooch
3. Good Boy
4. Isle of Dogs is stop-motion so probably not what you're looking for, but definitely check out Turner & Hooch and Marley & Me if you haven't seen them yet
5. Far From Home : The Adventures of Yellow Dog (1995)

Red Dog (2011)
6. K9 with Jim Belushi.
7. Stray (2020)

It's a documentary following a stray dog in Istanbul. It's incredible
8. Wild Fang

The Journey of Natty Gann

(both featuring the same dog actor, Jed, who was also in The Thing!)
9. Will definitely watch Turner & Hooch. Marley & Me watched. Thanks.
10. The Art of Racing in the Rain

## 3. Big blizzard coming, time for blizzard movies

**Score:** 19
**Body:** Looking for New England-set (or northeastern US generally), bonus points for accents and/or specific regional quirks. Would love a horror, thriller, or maybe like a noir kinda vibe. Let’s assemble a blizzard-themed film list! 

I’ve recently watched Day After Tomorrow, love all things Stephen King, and I also enjoyed that season of American Horror Story with the nor’easter episode. 

Any other good blizzard pics?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Ice Storm (1997)
  - I wish I could upvote this 100 times.
2. The Lighthouse (2019)

Shutter Island (2010)

The Fog (1980)

The Thing (1982) (antarctica, but a classic)

Insomnia (2002) (alaska, but i think the feel is there)

Misery (1990) is another obvious option but you already know about that
  - Misery and Fargo are the best but neither are Northeast/New England.
3. Stephen King has a great one that fits to a tee  STORM OF THE CENTURY

Carpenter's THE THING is not New England but is very wintery
4. Ang Lee's The Ice Storm checks a lot of boxes - very New England and a riveting drama!
5. Fargo

The Thing
6. Not a movie, but since you mentioned American Horror Story, I’ll take it as permission to recommend “The Terror” on Netflix- the first season is fantastic and a perfect snow watch.
7. The Hateful Eight
8. Hundreds of Beavers

## 4. Looking for movies about people who love music

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Like the title says, I’m looking for movies about (or its a big part of the movie anyway) people who love music. Movies where a character’s (or multiple characters) love of music is central. Any genre and any time period is fine. I’m very grateful to anyone who recommends, so thank you. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. High Fidelity (2000)
2. Almost Famous
3. Empire Records
4. Mr Hollands Opus. 
5. School of rock (2003)
6. - Whiplash (2014)
7. Sing Street

The Commitments
8. Once

Begin Again

Eurovision: The Story of Fire Saga
9. Pirate Radio
10. Amadeus 1984

## 5. Looking for crime/mystery movies for my 94 year old grandma

**Score:** 18
**Body:** I’m looking for some recommendations for my 94 year old grandma. She loves to read anything crime/mystery but has been having a hard time reading her books lately. I’m hoping maybe I can find a some good movies that would entertain her. Something with a compelling story line but somewhat easy to follow and nothing with too much violence. 

If anyone has any ideas they would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Forget films. Colombo. 90+ episodes of telly gold.
2. The Poirot movies (not the recent remakes) might be good for her. And Clue is really fun.
3. Not a movie, but my mom is also a crime/mystery fan, and she loves the new Matlock show with Kathy Bates.
4. Matlock and Columbo is al she needs.
5. Knives Out or Any Agatha Christie adaptation
6. My 89 year old mom LOVES cozy mysteries from the UK (they stream on several major platforms). I'll list some of her favorites. (I love them too)

Agatha Christie’s Seven Dials. This just came out on Netflix this week and we loved it A stylish period mystery with wit and intrigue, full of eccentric characters and classic Christie twists.

Poirot. Agatha Christie’s famous Belgian detective solves clever, talk-heavy mysteries with elegance, gentle humor, and no graphic content. The movies are good but nobody does Poirot better than David Suchet. 

Miss Marple. Cozy village mysteries where an unassuming elderly woman quietly outsmarts everyone using sharp observation and common sense. There is the original and then they did some remakes in the 2010s. And the old black and white ones are great too.

Murdoch Mysteries. A turn-of-the-century Canadian detective series that blends clever cases, light romance, gentle comedy, and early forensic science.

The Brokenwood Mysteries. Set in a small New Zealand town where quirky locals and dry humor balance out thoughtful, puzzle-driven murders.

Midsomer Murders. Classic English countryside mysteries with charming villages, eccentric suspects, and a comforting, familiar rhythm. (The way the murders are carried out are crazy but that is definitely a big part of the fun!)

Father Brown. A priest in 1950s England solves crimes using empathy, moral insight, and quiet intelligence rather than force.

Sister Boniface Mysteries. A brilliant nun with a love of science solves crimes with warmth, humor, and period charm.

Grantchester. A 1950s village mystery series pairing a thoughtful vicar with a gruff detective, mixing gentle humor with emotional depth. There have been 3 Vicars so far and they are all good in different ways.

The Madame Blanc Mysteries. A light, cozy mystery set in the South of France featuring an antiques expert solving crimes with charm and warmth.

Rosemary & Thyme. Two middle-aged women who run a gardening business stumble into murders, offering comfort-watch mysteries with soft humor.

Shakespeare & Hathaway. Modern-day Stratford-upon-Avon mysteries with theatrical flair, witty banter, and cozy British charm.Their.assistant steals the show in every episode. Lol

Death in Paradise. A sunny island setting with gentle humor, lovable detectives, and classic whodunit structure that never feels heavy. There have been 4 detectives and several other cast changes but they are all wonderful.

The Chelsea Detective. A calm, character-driven London mystery focused more on people and relationships than violence.

Hetty Wainthropp Investigates. A sharp-minded older woman runs a detective agency with her younger assistant, full of heart and gentle comedy.
7. Thursday Murder Club
8. She may be familiar with it, but Sorry, Wrong Number
9. The Name of the Rose (1986)
10. Poirot Misteries
Father Brown Misteries 
Sister Bonafice Misteries 

Easy cheese, but fun to watch.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Best response to a threat in a movie. I’ll start: Lucius Fox to Reese in The Dark Knight.

**Score:** 3755

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Vinnie Jones and his Desert Eagle in Snatch (2000) : "and the fact that you have "replica" written on the side of your gun..."
  - That whole interaction is so good
2. This from Happy Gilmore:

"I eat pieces of shit like you for breakfast".

"You eat pieces of shit for breakfast?"

(Long pause) "No!"
3. Haha this scene was hilarious and probably in reality would have been used several times
  - It becomes an open secret that Bruce is Batman, but nobody says anything in public to avoid a lawsuit, and as a result Bruce has no idea everyone knows.
    - There's an old SNL sketch where Clark Kent is working at the Daily Bugle. The entire office knows, but he keeps the charade going

Can't find the full sketch, though https://youtube.com/shorts/Fxa5EYgmdAw
4. "None of you seem to understand. I'm not locked in here with you. You're locked in here with ME!" - Rorschach from Watchmen.
5. It's not a movie, but there is a scene in justified that i think is one of the most badass threats.
The main character, Raylen, is threatening a recurring bad guy, Duffy, who has been thrown to the ground and is held at gun point. 
They exchange words, and then Raylen pulls the slide back on his gun, injecting a shell, throws it at duffy, and simply say...
"Next ones coming at you faster."

Lives rent-free in my mind
6. In Tombstone, Wyatt Earp hits Curly Bill after Bill kills the town marshal. The Cowboy gang begin surrounding him, and Ike Clanton tells Wyatt to let him go or else. But as Ike says they'll tear him apart, Wyatt presses the barrel of his gun against Ike's forehead and says: "You die first, get it? Your friends might get me in a rush, but not before I make your head into a canoe, you understand me?"
7. I love in Phone Booth where the sniper tells Colin Farrell’s character that he has a “30-calibre bolt-action 700 with a Carbon One modification and a state-of-the-art Hensoldt tactical scope. And it's staring straight at you” and his response is “yeah, well how’s my fucking hair?”

## 2. Am I the only one who thinks Sinners is overrated?

**Score:** 1439
**Body:** I'm not saying it's bad! But so many people are saying that I'm surprised!
I liked the movie... I just didn't find it masterful, but it's good.
However, I'm sure the movie will lose in several Oscar categories... at most, it won't win Best Original Score.
It barely has a chance in Best Picture.
Direction too. Acting is the same thing... Michael B. Jordan won't win the Best Actor Oscar, and Wunmi Mosaku won't be nominated, and in the technical categories, there are many films superior to it!
The same goes for Original Song... "I Lied to You" won't win, I think the song is very overrated even though I loved it!
In short, I think the movie will be snubbed in several categories and lose in almost all of them!

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. No one is the only one of anything.
  - whoa
    - Dust. Wind. Dude.
      - So-crates?
        - I believe he’s actually Dave Beeth Oven
  - I’m the only one with this username on Reddit
    - Saturday I just watched the movie again for the 9th time.. and I said your name in to existence. And today here your are.
2. The movie is great but the fans of the movie overrate it. I saw someone today saying that it changed the trajectory of cinema and that sinners is a generational masterpiece.
  - Holy shit lol I love this movie but that is some fart smelling nonsense.
3. I feel it was good and it stuck with me afterwards, but I don’t see it as a masterpiece.

## 3. Happy 70th birthday to Geena Davis!! Do you have a favorite character she played?

**Score:** 480
**Body:** I’ll start. Mrs. Little in the Stuart Little movies 

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Long Kiss Goodnight - fun movie
  - Gina was amazing in this, and Sam Jackson....chefs kiss
    - Brian Cox ruled majorly, too.
2. Can’t go wrong with Beetlejuice
3. Dottie Hinson. Top 5 baseball player in movie history.
4. Hard to pick. Favorite character arc: Thelma and Louise.
5. Mrs. Little and Dottie in A League of Their Own.
6. The Fly
7. League of Their Own. Classic movie
8. She was a stunner in A League of Their Own.

## 4. Mercy (2026) Chris Pratt's career worst?

**Score:** 402
**Body:** Now I didn't for one minute expect a masterpiece going into Mercy, but the sheer level of dross served up reaches levels of insulting. The term "phoned it in" would be a nice way of putting it for Chris Pratt, but, I'll be fair to him, the material he's given is up there for some of the worst.

Full review is here for those interested: [https://adamreviewsfilm.com/mercy/](https://adamreviewsfilm.com/mercy/) 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Worst **so far**
2. I understand that no biopic is ever going to have absolutely perfect casting, but Chris Pratt as Stephen Hawking? Hard pass.
  - ~~No fucking way. This is not a Stephen Hawking movie staring Chris Pratt, you have to be joking.~~  

You were being facetious, and I can’t tell what’s real anymore. Can’t wait to see Pratt star in the Bible 2.
    - 2 Bible 2 Furious
3. Christ Pratt has a fairly extensive (and growing) library of worsts.
4. My vibe from the trailer:

Someone mixed 2 parts Minority Report with one part Hardcore Henry... Added a dash of Internet vigilante wish fulfillment

Then ran it thru the in-house AI at temu a few times
  - When I first saw it I literally went, is this just supposed to be a new version of minority report?
5. Isn't this just that movie with Ice Cube but with Chris Pratt AND Rebecca Ferguson??
  - Funnily enough the guy who directed this produced War of the Worlds
6. The trailer just made this movie seem so fucking dumb, my god. Who pays for this shit?

## 5. Margot Robbie and Jacob Elordi In Wuthering Heights (2026)

**Score:** 360

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Me when I see my dog chewing something he shouldn't be.
  - I hope your dog isn't giving the same reaction as hers
    - Don’t kink shame my dog, please.
2. Fifty shades of beige.

☠️
3. “Yes, I believe I can feel a cavity starting right here.”
  - And that’s with the *other* hand.
4. No running water, traveling for hours and hours on dirt roads with horses and sucking on A MAN'S fingers.....istg shenanigans like this is how europeans got the fucking plague 😷
5. I no longer remembered that passage in the book
6. He is Kenough.
7. For a book about a sickly dude this seems like a great way to spread germs

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Kagemusha (1980), identity, and being greater

**Score:** 14
**Body:** Kagemusha is one of my favourite films. It is a complex tapestry that only a director like Kurosawa could weave. It is simultaneously a grand epic and a deeply personal character study that moved me on a deep level. The Shadow Warrior and his relationship with Lord Shingen's grandson is one of the most touching and tragic relationships I have ever seen on film, and its conclusion asks something of the audience: was the fiction of the Shadow Warrior, the relationship he built with the real Shingen's grandson, more important than the truth of who he really was? I believe that this film shows how one can grow and be a better person from the simple love of another, even if that love is built around falsehood. The Shadow Warrior at the beginning of the movie is no one, and by the end is no one again, but in between, he is a kind and caring grandfather, a noble and thoughtful leader, and underneath it all, a man who only needed love to show his true colours.

  
can the shadow of a man stand up and walk on its own? With love, maybe it can.

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 2. When physical pain becomes the subject of narrative cinema.

**Score:** 9
**Body:** I’ve been thinking a lot about how cinema handles internal, invisible experiences like chronic pain or neurological suffering.

In my own work, I found that traditional realism completely failed to express what that experience feels like from the inside. I ended up turning to non-literal tools. hand-painted animation, abstraction, and fragmentation layered over live action- not to explain pain, but to *translate* it.

I’m curious how others here feel about this:  
**Where do you think narrative cinema succeeds or fails when it tries to depict experiences that have no clear visual language?**

Are there films you think genuinely manage to make internal states *felt*, not just described?

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Ingmar Bergman's *Cries and Whispers* immediately comes to mind as a film that deals with both physical and emotional pain in a powerful way. The mise-en-scene and editing play a big part in creating that effect.
2. It's documentary, rather than narrative, but this is a fascinating look at the subject:

[Sick: The Life an Death of Bob Flanagan, Supermasochist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sick:_The_Life_and_Death_of_Bob_Flanagan,_Supermasochist)

## 3. Do documentary films get enough recognition?

**Score:** 2
**Body:** I have been working on a project of mine to find the best film of every year (since the 1920s), and I have been eyeing Dziga Vertov's *Man with a Movie Camera* since I developed an interest in film and just yesterday, I finally watched it. I had never seen any film like it before, I am not sure if I can describe the film well, but it was all about how reality can be captured in film and the power of filmmaking itself.  
But now having seen *Man with a Movie Camera*, I got thinking about documentary films. *Man with a Movie Camera* is one of the most celebrated films by a fair bit of critics, and it is regarded a documentary. But besides *Man with a Movie Camera,* I do not think many documentary get a lot of recognition in a lot of film discourse.  
I feel like documentary films are barely acknowledged in a lot of film discourse, at least the discourse that I see, they rather centre on fictional narrative films. I was able to find some really good documentaries to watch, but I needed to look for them much harder than for narrative films.  
I personally think that documentary films deserve a lot more recognition, or what if had I just been too much around spaces that favour narrative films over documentaries?

What do you think?

Edit: I am asking this because I do not feel very knowledgeable on the topic of documentary films, and I PERSONALLY have not seen much discussion of it. I was hoping to get some more insight by making this post.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think some of the most interesting films are things like essay films and other films that straddle the line, mixing fiction and nonfiction. Films like On the Bowery or Savage Eye
  - That's interesting. This has reminded me of a film I once heard of, I do not remember what it was called, but it was a WW2 film, it had a story, but the main focus was that a lot of footage that was used in the film was actual archived WW2 footage, but I cannot remember what it was called.
2. Documentary film really goes back to the invention of motion pictures. Robert Flaherty - just to use one example in only one country - hit big with *Nanook of the North* in 1922. Not only were many early films the result of cameras turned to reality, but also audiences in theaters (once they were built) would have been exposed to documentary films of all types, as parts of longer, multi-part "programs" in the moviegoing experience that we don't have, anymore.
  - Nanook of the North was all made up (for what it's worth)
3. You need to see Man With a Movie Camera re-scored by the Cinematic Orchestra.

Other than that I have no meaningful reply to your conceit. Documentary is as respected as it needs to be. 

Good luck with your project.
  - I did not mean to come off as vain, I made this post because I have personally not seen much acknowledgement of them, and wanted to see what other people thought, in hope to gain new insight.
But thank you for your reply.
    - You didn't come across as vain.
I applaud your project.
But definitely try and track down that version. I have a dvd promo copy and saw it performed live back in the day. But I think it's available online quite easily now. It's the best version for me.
      - Oh, well then pardon me, I misunderstood the meaning of "conceit". I just feel like my understanding of film is not be as deep as I would like it to and I do not want to act like it is.  
I will definitely look for the rescoring of *Man with a Movie Camera* you mentioned, thank you for the recommendation.

## 4. The Problem With Eddington

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I will admit than when I first saw Eddington, I felt very annoyed by it. Initially, I felt like it was taking an incoherent centrist position and not really saying anything of substantive value. As more time passes, I think I have softened on it a little bit, but I still think it is a seriously flawed film and here I am going to explain why.

One thing that annoyed me about the film was the contrast in it's portrayal between leftists and conservatives. Just to be clear, I think this film presents conservatives as being far more dangerous than leftists. My problem is the film seems to devote much more energy to mocking leftists than it does to mocking conservatives. It feels like half the movie is mocking leftist teenagers for being annoying and woke, while not nearly as much time is spent mocking the right wing characters. I think this contributes to making the film feel centrist when in actuality it is not. For me, this is why I think the satire in One Battle After Another landed so much more effectively than the satire in Eddington.

Another reason I believe so many people call Eddington centrist, is because it takes on a lot of very controversial and hot button political topics like Black Lives Matter and mask mandates, but It doesn't really say anything substantive about them. This is because that even if these topics represent a large part of the plot of Eddington, fundamentally the film is not actually about them. Eddington is about how isolation and the internet can create violent divisions between people and how the pandemic exacerbated these divisions. It is also about how elite forces take advantage of these divisions. All the stuff about BLM and mask mandates is just set dressing that is not all that relevant to the core themes of the film.

I think that there are a lot of problems with this approach. In my opinion if you make protests against police brutality and systemic racism a large part of the plot of your film, you are obligated to say something about these topics. If you don't, it ends of feeling dismissive of a very real issue.  This was not helped by the fact that our entire representation of the movement in the film was performative white people. I don't deny that there were lots of performative white people in the BLM protests, but in my opinion it feels wrong that we did not get a single black person participating in the BLM protests. It made it feel like the film was portraying the protests as a bunch of woke posturing rather than a protest against a real systemic issue. I don't know if this was intentional, but it still left a very bad taste in my mouth.

All this together causes the film to intentionally or unintentionally play into a very flawed worldview. A common idea I have seen expressed by a lot of people on the left and even some people on the right is that the real conflict in America is not between right and left but rather between the lower class and the elites and that "woke" cultural issues are a distraction. In Eddington, the true conflict in the film is the data center being built, all the other insane culture war stuff is irrelevant. 

The problem with this worldview is that it just isn't true.  Class fundamentally cannot be separated from racism, patriarchy, homophobia, transphobia and all those other "woke" cultural issues. They are all tied up together in one big system. If you try to solve class without solving any of those other issues it will be completely ineffective. Therein lies the problem with Eddington. Its complete dismissal of BLM and other "woke" issues make It's eventual critique of the elites of society completely ineffective.  One could even interpret it as playing into right wing talking points that "wokeness" is something associated with the elites. After all, at the beginning of the film Garcia is for the data center while Cross is against it.  

To be clear, I don't think the film is right wing. I rather think it is simply ineffectively left wing. I also don't even think its bad.  It's one of the few films I've seen that really captures how our current political climate feels and It communicates its message about online radicalization and isolation very effectively. I think the problem comes when it ties that message to real concrete political issues. I think that the way it went about doing this caused it to reinforce some reactionary class reductionist ideas. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I view the film as fundamentally materialist, and from that perspective it mocks the futility of the American Left in the context of late stage capitalism and the unreality age we are living in rather than mocking leftist positions. I don’t think thats centrist.
2. The fact that so many people see so many messages in Eddington confirms (for me anyway) that it is an actually great movie. And while great art may have an underlying motivation,  it's effect can be much more broad.
3. The film is effectively left wing. It expounds upon the pervasive corporate influence in America and despite what they might present to the public, American politicians answer to the same masters. How many mayors or congresspersons have sold out their constituents in the name of “economic development”. Both the right wing characters and “left” wing characters were slaves to the facades they presented not out of belief, but to consolidate power. When they achieved that power (in Phoenix’s case through violent force, not sure how a more damning critique could be made) they ultimately made the same decisions.
4. you think the movie about a completely unadulteratedly moronic, bumbling, man-child simpleton cop, who uses his petty grievances as the launching point of an entirely unserious and cynical conservative political platform and later externalizes his personal issues by violently murdering four people, including a child, is more Left-critical because it poked fun at a subsection of White BLM teens?
5. You keep referring to “leftists” in the film, and I would posit that there are no “leftists” in the film at all, and that should lead you to a different understanding.  Also, I think if you criticize Aster for not addressing BLM in the proper tone and context that you desired going into the movie, you’re actually just wishing that he made a different film then the one he wanted and actually made.  I don’t think he has to make a solemn pronouncement on The Meaning of BLM in order to make his own characters seem superficial, it’s actually just a movie about these characters, that’s all.
6. The film is not even close to centrist. 

The BLM portion of the film displayed Joe's disregard for the movement. As well as the townsfolk calling in a "riot" which was just a peaceful protest. We don't even know who threw the chair through the window. But the auto assumption was that it was the BLM protestors. The film obviously displayed the Republicans complete disregard of the movements ideology as well as painting the movement as violent. 

The mask mandate displayed how Joe threw his power around to demand entrance in the grocery store. As though mask mandates don't apply to him. Which is what Republicans did. 

Ari Aster calls himself rather far left. To call Eddington moderate is a misreading of the film.
7. I think you need to watch it again. My favorite film of the year in a strong field

Its definitely filmed from the viewpoint of Phoenix’s character so it’s reflective of his thought process as he plunges down the rabbit hole of conspiracy nonsense

Being forced to wear a mask is an existential threat which triggers the spiral to the point he has to kill the mayor

Suddenly BLM and teenagers protesting is a riot in his mind not 10 people blocking a street with no traffic 

ANTIFA is an assassination squad trained and equipped like an elite military force

He’s overwhelmed by annoyances be amplified into crisis by social media while ignoring m/minimizing the two real threats, the mental homeless guy with COVID and the data center which will bleed the town dry

And I’m aware skipping over a lot of the other symbolism in it 
8. “Class fundamentally cannot be separated from racism, patriarchy, homophobia, transphobia and all those other "woke" cultural issues.”

Disagree. You could have an all white all straight cis community with perfect gender equality and you would still have class issues.
9. I thought Eddington was a lot smarter and more nuanced politically than OBAA, which did pretty cartoonish caricatures of right wing types. It’s not a better film mind you, but Ari is at least more cognizant of the current political climate. And I am not a conservative in any way, shape, or form.
  - I've never thought this was much of a fair comparison. I think the difference is more that Edddington is much more directly a film "about" politics, whereas OBAA isn't "about" politics as much as it uses political struggle as a setting in which to place its story.

## 5. In the movie “Eyes Wide Shut” Was Bill really fragile, or was his wife lying?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** I just got done watching the movie “Eyes Wide Shut” after being recommended this movie as if it was some masterclass on writing conspiracies and high tension moments.

Many scenes started and stopped abruptly, panned on for far too long, or could’ve been removed and nothing at all would have been lost.

I often found myself being completely confused on the character motivations for a lot of characters.

  
Let’s start with Bill’s (Tom Cruise) wife (Nicole Kidman). They get back from the party where they both “almost” cheat on eachother, but they end up just flirting and leaving. 

Then Nicole starts to smoke and she obviously feels insecure about the two women who seemed to be after the doctor.  

Bill then reassures her, and says he didn't cheat. she moves the goalpost to him being a medical malpractitioner, and asks if he’s certain that his female clients don’t desire him sexually. 

He dismisses the idea on the basis that they’re women, and therefore their minds don’t go there, (which obviously ignores that women are people too, not just objects to put on a pedestal). 

This causes his wife to fly into a fit of rage and hysterics, to ”reveal” the ”evil true nature” of women. She does this by revealing she “almost” had an affair with some captain of a cruise she went on a while ago. 

She goes into brutal detail about how much she desires him. 

That was the long way around to ask, why the fuck did she do that?! What was the point? She was just at the party soliciting attention and leading that guy on as much as possible, why come home and attack her husband with a pointless story of another guy giving you butterflies?!? 

The only possible explanation is that this was a confession. Not just of her intentions but of what she actually did. That she had cheated already, and wanted to get it off her chest without actually doing so and taking accountability.

Now i initially had one thought when i heard the story she told. “Oh she definitely isn’t telling the full truth, she cheated on him.” 

But it seems everyone else believes that her story is actually the truth?! And she just cruelly for no reason whatsoever confesses her desire to cheat on her husband?!?

Women can be cruel yes, but usually there is a REASON. This was blatant disrespect and if Bill respected himself he would’ve left her.

Let’s take it a step further. In this movie I’m supposed to believe that Bill thinks his wife fantasized about another guy and thats why he tried to cheat on her not once, not twice, but three times??? And is thwarted EVERY time?

That makes no sense!! He has to be either incredibly fragile, or he understands that she really did sleep with the doctor.

This also would explain why so far into the movie the wife has a dream of a scene similar to the party, because her conscious is revealing who she really is. 

I could go on and on but that’s my question. Was the entire driving force of the movie bills insane response to disrespect from his wife? or was my initial read correct and his wife actually cheated on him?

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The reason is she’s already feeling hurt by him abandoning her to flirt with two random women (she is only entertaining the flirty dude hitting on her, and only so drunk, because she was waiting for her husband who didn’t show up after ages and ages but did appear before her with other women in another room).

And then she’s insulted by this idea that women are muted little objects with no desires of their own.

So she needles him back, to teach him a lesson (both in the sense of punishing him, and in the sense of educating him out of his ignorant view of women as merely reactive in their sexuality). It’s petty, it’s cruel, but it’s what she feels like doing in her place of hurt and insult. She lays it on far thicker than necessary to get a rise out of him, as some couples with bad conflict skills often do. 
2. Two points:

1. The film was notoriously changed post-production in the editing room. You noticed that there are a lot of dangling threads with unanswered questions? Yea, that's why.
2. Like any Kubrick film, there is an unhealthy degree of conspiracizing about the film. Doesn't help that the film literally features a sex-cult.

The movie is about access to power. Tom Cruise feels that as a man, he has an inherent right to power. He proposes this notion to his wife under the idea of sexual liberty, she instantly spurns him and provides an example of her own ability to cheat.. if she wanted to. Did she cheat? Yes, no, maybe. It doesn't matter. The point is that she can.

Cruise spends the rest of the night going on an Alice in Wonderland journey to realize just how out of touch with the world he really is (told through his innocence with relation to sex). No matter how wealthy or close to powerful people he gets, he will never be invited into the real halls of power.
  - A significant visual clue in regards to your final point is bathroom size. Bill and Alice have a great apartment in NYC and no doubt the mortgage or rent would be astronomical in terms of any middle class or lower class budget. Yet they seem to have one bathroom and it is small and cramped. Compare that with Ziegler's bathroom where Bill finds himself at the party in the first act. Ziegler likely has more than just that one bathroom in his spacious house as well. It tells a very large story just in that detail.
  - Well said, love all of this. Thank you.
3. Kubrick adapted the film from a 1926 Austrian novella set in Vienna at Mardi Gras—thus the colorful conceit of a masquerade that conceals an orgy, and (to your point) the protagonist’s fragile ego and easily shocked naïveté about women having sexual desires.  

Kubrick had wanted to make a film based on the book since the late 1960s, and the plot and characters follow the source material very closely (much more so than, for example, *The Shining*), so the conspiracist notions of it being a commentary on recent events is silly.  Given its 1920s setting, it is unlikely that the wife was intended to be dishonest.  It is also worth noting that Kubrick cast a couple of Hollywood’s best-looking actors as leads, whereas in the book, it is implied that the older doctor and his younger wife are an ordinary, respectable Jewish couple. Tom Cruise’s uncertainty as the doctor is originally in the novella a sort of stuffy officiousness.   (This only survives in the movie as his arrogant “I’m a doctor” insistence that people let him into their business.)
4. Your question reminds me of a somewhat apocryphal story about two famous writers, one of whom may have been Hemingway. Apparently the other writer’s wife disparaged her husband’s ‘size’ in mixed company, so Hemingway dragged him off to the bathroom, checked him and told him ‘he’s fine’. 

Some women really care about cock size, other realise that it can be a way to mess with their man’s insecurities, for better or worse. Kubrick was renowned for opening up cans of worms without ever revealing why or what for. *Eyes Wide Shut* in particular has a lot of this, especially as it focuses a lot on personal sexual politics and how frail we really are, even in the sanctity of marriage.
  - I’ll add to the above by mentioning an old friend who got a new girlfriend who apparently told him that he’s huge. He was on cloud nine for weeks, couldn’t stop telling everyone about it. His close group of friends advised him to calm down a bit and not take it to heart.

Anyway, months later they inevitably break up. And of course in the heat of all the arguments she tells him that he’s actually fucking tiny. He’s absolutely mortified, couldn’t stop telling everyone about it. His close group of friends advised him to calm down a bit and not take it to heart.
5. Yes. And they are both unreliable narrators, blinded by their own perceptual biases and insecurities.

I feel like *Eyes Wide Shut* is more important for opening the door for movies like it to follow, more so than it was a great film in and of itself. I don’t think we get A24 without it. The moody/high brow conceptual/incredibly intimate flick that is focused mostly on storytelling through vibes is something Kubrick was really at the forefront of.

If you’re interested in it more, read up on the controversy around the timing of Kubrick’s death with the production. Many feel like he was still figuring out the pacing of the film but since he died, no one really wanted to touch up the latest cut he had made. Some of the cast and crew have conflicting perspectives though, with some stating that Kubrick made it clear he was “close” to a final cut. Others saying he felt like he had just got started with the editing process. I feel like this “debate” is a great microcosm of the film’s themes.
  - FWIW the film’s editor, Nigel Galt, strongly intimated in a recent interview that what we got was basically what Kubrick intended. Before he died they had to do housekeeping edits like establishing shot inserts and minor pacing amendments, but it sounds like the idea that anything major was left out is fanciful.

As you say, very in keeping with the conspiratorial aura of the film itself.
6. My interpretation based on podcasts and articles about the film is that Kubrick didn’t really know what he wanted. He was famously a perfectionist, but his endless filming of Eyes Wide Shut seems more like someone who’s trying everything in the hope that something will eventually stick. 

It’s a very unfocused film, so it’s easy for people to put their own interpretations on it. However none of those interpretations feel all that satisfying or convincing to me.
